title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Type 1 Prices Rise Slightly with a Rise in Trading Volumes
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(31st March â€“ 06th April)
  • A total of 426,734 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 7.90% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades also witnessed a rise of 73.90% WoW as 3,085,632 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price rose this week to $59.17/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 0.70%Â rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 9.73% in price, reaching $66.87/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 2.29% WoW; on 08th April, the spot price stood at $61.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $64.63/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose 2.03% WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 15.38% WoW each along with D3 RIN prices rising by 16.67% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

On April 4, 2025, CARB released its Third 15-Day Package for the LCFS regulation following the Office of Administrative Lawâ€™s February rejection, introducing revisions aimed at enhancing clarity and consistency without altering the ambitious CI reduction targets set for 2025. The market responded positively, with spot prices jumping from $56.50/MT to $62.00/MT, fueled by speculation that the benchmarks may still apply from Q1 if the regulation is finalized by June 30. Key updates include increased support for hydrogen refueling infrastructureâ€”especially in the heavy-duty segmentâ€”through a higher derating factor and removal of credit caps tied to capital expenditure. Additionally, the regulation now allows hydrogen produced with carbon capture and sequestration (CCS) to count toward the renewable hydrogen target and exempts it from the 2035 fossil hydrogen phaseout. All base credits will now go to EDUs, excluding OEMs, with a portion directed to the Clean Fuel Reward program. Land-use change (LUC) calculations have been standardized using satellite data and empirical feedstock yields, and stricter rules now govern how missing data can be handled in LCFS reporting. For further details, read here.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_595024" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.70% WoW rise, increasing from $58.76 to $59.17 this week, while all non-zero credit prices increased by 9.73% WoW, from $60.94 to $66.87. Type 1 trades saw 426,734 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 7.90% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a rise of 73.90% to reach 3,085,632 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_595026" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 2.29% WoW, reaching $57.70/MT. On 8th April, the average spot price was $61.00/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $64.63/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_595027" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 130 trades were reported in the market, with 27 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $166.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 12,500 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.75 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 20,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 40 credits of the Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $56.00. The largest volume transfers of 300,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for prices of $66.17 and $74.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_595028" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In March, the average price of Spot Type 1 credits dropped to $58.44, down from $72.15 in February, reflecting a period of market uncertainty. However, early signs of recovery have emerged in April, with the average price climbing slightly to $59.26 in the first week. Trading activity has picked up notably as wellâ€”volumes in March totaled 5.31 million, while the first week of April alone has already seen 2.79 million credits traded, surpassing half of March's total volume. This uptick suggests renewed confidence and momentum in the market following CARBâ€™s latest regulatory update.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_595029" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 31st March â€“ 06th April, total VPT recorded a 1.30x times rise (WoW), with a rise in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose 2.03% WoW. D4 and D5 RIN prices increased by 15.38% WoW each alongwith D3 RIN prices rising by 16.67% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • ECCC Updates Clean Fuel Regulations Guidance. ECCCâ€™s Low Carbon Fuels Division has released an updated factsheet on the Clean Fuel Regulations (CFR), providing additional guidance on credit creation and tracking, as well as annual adjustments under Section 89 and carbon-intensity (CI) pathway reports. Additionally, ECCC reminds stakeholders of the April 30th, 2025 deadline for submitting the Material Balance Report and its verification, with an accompanying factsheet available for reference.
  • Illinois Increases Biodiesel Blend Rate to B17. A bipartisan bill passed in 2022 to promote the use of higher biodiesel blends is making a significant impact once again in 2025. Effective today, April 1, biodiesel blend requirements will increase from B14 to B17, marking another step toward advancing cleaner fuel options. Illinois Soybean Association (ISA) Chairman and Illinois soybean farmer Ron Kindred is proud to see the continuation of efforts that support our stateâ€™s farmers. â€œIncreasing biodiesel blend rates is a win for farmers, consumers and the environment,â€ said Kindred. In 2026, the minimum biodiesel blend levels eligible for a tax exemption jump to 20%.
  • Assemblywoman Soria Introduces EV-School Bus Extension Bill, Providing more Time and Flexibility Ahead of Electric Fleet Deadline. Assemblywoman Esmeralda Soria (D-Merced) is announcing her bill AB 1111 today, which seeks to address challenges that local educational agencies (LEAs) face in adopting zero-emission school buses by 2035. AB 1111 extends the deadline to 2045, makes changes to existing exemptions to consider infrastructure and fire safety concerns, and provides flexibility to an existing school bus scrapping program to provide buses for challenged districts.
  • Fuel Groups Warn EPA: High Biofuel Mandates Could Spike Diesel Costs. The letter from NACS, NATSO, and SIGMA to EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin raises concerns over the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS) and advanced biofuel mandates. They warn that high blending targets, combined with the expiration of the $1.00 per gallon Biodiesel Tax Credit (BTC), could drive up diesel costs, impacting inflation and the economy. The replacement 45Z tax credit incentivizes production, not consumption, failing to lower fuel prices. With biofuel plants shutting down and imports declining, they urge the EPA to set realistic mandates to avoid economic strain while expressing willingness to collaborate on energy policies.
  • Senators Thune, Durbin Ask Trump to Permit the Nationwide, Year-Round Sale of E15 Fuel.S. Sens. John Thune (R-S.D.) and Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) led a bipartisan group of their colleagues in asking President Trump to permitthe year-round sale of E15 fuel through the summer of 2025. The senatorsâ€™ bipartisan letter requests that President Trump use the nationwide temporary waivers provided under the Clean Air Act to extend the Reid vapor pressure waiver through the 2025 summer driving season.
  • USDA Announces Release of Obligated Funds of $537 Million Under Higher Blends Infrastructure Incentive Program (HBIIP). U.S. Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins announced the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) will release obligated funding under the Higher Blends Infrastructure Incentive Program (HBIIP) for 543 projects totaling $537 million in 29 states. The purpose of the HBIIP is to increase significantly the sales and use of higher blends of ethanol and biodiesel by expanding the infrastructure for renewable fuels derived from U.S. agricultural products.
  • US Oil and Biofuel Coalition Meets with EPA to Seek Biomass Diesel Boost. A U.S. oil and biofuel coalition met with the Environmental Protection Agency on Tuesday to make the case for higher federal mandates for biomass diesel blending, according to three sources familiar with the plans. The EPA meeting signals that the agency may be closer to releasing fresh biofuel blending quotas for the Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS). The proposal is expected to cover two years worth of mandates, two sources familiar with the matter told Reuters. The EPA could release the proposal as early as this month, sources said.
  • Senators Thune, Durbin Ask Trump to Permit the Nationwide, Year-Round Sale of E15 Fuel. U.S. Sens. John Thune (R-S.D.) and Dick Durbin (D-Ill.) led a bipartisan group of their colleagues in asking President Trump to permitthe year-round sale of E15 fuel through the summer of 2025. The senatorsâ€™ bipartisan letter requests that President Trump use the nationwide temporary waivers provided under the Clean Air Act to extend the Reid vapor pressure waiver through the 2025 summer driving season. â€œTo meet our nationâ€™s energy needs and decrease the cost of fuel, we must deploy an all-of-the-above energy strategy, which includes leveraging domestic biofuels,â€ the senators wrote.
